title: Delete
subtitle: d is the verb. Pair it with any motion.
summary: d is the delete operator. It takes a motion: dw deletes a word, d$ deletes to end of line. Doubling the operator (dd) makes it linewise. x and X are convenience deletes for single characters.

blocks.text: {key:d} is the *delete operator*. It's a verb that needs a noun. The noun is any motion or text object. {key:d}{key:w} deletes a word. {key:d}{key:$} deletes to end of line. {key:d}{key:i}{key:p} deletes the inner paragraph. The grammar carries you here.

blocks.caption: D — delete to end of line

blocks.text: Counts compose multiplicatively. {key:3}{key:d}{key:2}{key:w} deletes six words. The {key:3} multiplies the {key:2} multiplies the motion. Read it as "three times: delete two words."

blocks.title: Delete Doesn't Delete — It *Moves to a Register*
blocks.text:
When you press {key:d}{key:w}, Vim cuts the deleted text and stores it in **two** registers:

  1. The **unnamed register** ({key:"}{key:"}) — your most-recent yank-or-delete.
  2. A **numbered register** ({key:"}{key:1}–{key:"}{key:9}) — a rolling buffer of the last nine deletes.

So {key:p} after {key:d}{key:w} pastes the word back. {key:"}{key:1}{key:p} pastes the most-recent delete. {key:"}{key:2}{key:p} the one before. **Delete is just "cut."** This is also why deleting and then pasting elsewhere is the standard Vim move-text idiom.

The one register *not* touched by delete is the **yank register** ({key:"}{key:0}), which only updates on actual yanks. So if you yank, then delete, then paste, you get the *delete*. Use {key:"}{key:0}{key:p} to get the yank back. Full treatment: [`registers.numbered`](#registers.numbered) and [`registers.unnamed`](#registers.unnamed).

{key:D} = {key:d}{key:$}. {key:dd} (linewise delete) is the doubled-operator form. *Every* operator follows that pattern: a doubled operator means "act on this whole line." {key:c}{key:c}, {key:y}{key:y}, {key:>}{key:>}, {key:=}{key:=}, etc.
Curiosity: {key:x} and {key:X} are essentially {key:d}{key:l} and {key:d}{key:h} respectively. They exist for ergonomics — single-character deletes happen so often that they get one-key shortcuts.

blocks.title: Delete has two cursor-landing rules
blocks.text: Charwise delete leaves the cursor at the start of the deleted range. Linewise delete moves the cursor to the first non-blank of whatever line replaces the deletion. The post-delete landing is keyed off the deleted text's type tag ({key:d}{key:w} is charwise, {key:d}{key:d} is linewise), not off the operator key. That's why {key:d}{key:j} (linewise: deletes two lines) lands you on first-non-blank, but {key:d}{key:l} doesn't.
